Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 3: you have used 13 of about 34 target actions so far.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 8 actions returned the board to a state already seen 8 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 8 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Game over on the LEFT action from (3, 7) to (3, 5). The animation had 17 frames with the pattern '[9, 7, 7, 4, 3, 3, 17, 16, 15, 7, 7, 7, 7, 7, 8, 16, 8]'. The bounding box is 'top-left (22,24) 7x14, 54 cells', which covers grid rows 2-4 and cols 2-8 (approximately). This includes the red traps at (3, 3) and (3, 5), and the B cells at (2, 4), (2, 6), and (4, 6).
So the game over is caused by the LEFT animation from (3, 7) to (3, 5) passing near the red traps and B cells. The 17-frame animation is even longer than the usual 15 frames.
The pattern seems to be: horizontal moves that pass near red traps or B cells cause extended animations and game overs. But in level 2, the player successfully moved RIGHT from (4, 7) to (4, 9), which is near the red trap at (4, 7). Wait, no - the player was AT (4, 7), which is the red trap, and moved RIGHT to (4, 9). The animation for that move would include the red trap in the bounding box, but the game didn't end.
Let me think about this differently. In level 2:
- RIGHT from (4, 3) to (4, 5): 15 frames, game over. The bounding box includes the red trap at (4, 7).
- RIGHT from (4, 7) to (4, 9): 8 frames, no game over. The player is at the red trap, moving away from it.
In level 3:
- LEFT from (5, 7) to (5, 5): 15 frames, game over. The bounding box includes the B cell at (4, 6).
- LEFT from (3, 7) to (3, 5): 17 frames, game over. The bounding box includes the red traps at (3, 3) and (3, 5), and the B cells at (2, 4), (2, 6), and (4, 6).
So the pattern is: horizontal moves that pass towards red traps or B cells cause extended animations and game overs. But moves away from red traps or B cells are fine.
In level 2, the RIGHT from (4, 3) to (4, 5) is moving towards the red trap at (4, 7). The RIGHT from (4, 7) to (4, 9) is moving away from the red trap at (4, 7).
In level 3, the LEFT from (5, 7) to (5, 5) is moving towards the B cell at (4, 6) and the red traps at (3, 3) and (3, 5). The LEFT from (3, 7) to (3, 5) is moving towards the red traps at (3, 3) and (3, 5).
So the rule is: horizontal moves towards red traps or B cells cause game overs. Moves away from red traps or B cells are fine.
But wait, in level 2, the player successfully moved RIGHT from (6, 3) to (6, 5) and RIGHT from (6, 5) to (6, 7). These moves are not near any red traps or B cells, so they're fine.
In level 3, the player successfully moved LEFT from (3, 11) to (3, 9) and LEFT from (3, 9) to (3, 7). These moves are moving away from the right side, but they're not near any red traps or B cells. The red traps are at (3, 3) and (3, 5), and the B cells are at (2, 4), (2, 6), and (4, 6).
So the rule is: horizontal moves that pass within 1-2 grid cells of red traps or B cells cause game overs.
From (3, 7), the LEFT to (3, 5) passes within 1 grid cell of the red trap at (3, 5) and the B cell at (2, 6). So it causes a game over.
To avoid this, I need to find a path that doesn't have horizontal moves near red traps or B cells.
Looking at the grid:
- Row 5: W(-1), g(0), W(1), g(2), W(3), g(4), W(5), g(6), W(7), g(8), W(9), g(10), W(11)
- Row 7: R(-1), g(0), W(1), W(5), g(6), W(7), g(8), W(9)
From (9, 9), I can go:
- UP to (7, 9): valid
- UP to (5, 9): valid
- LEFT to (5, 7): valid (8 frames, no game over)
- LEFT to (5, 5): 15 frames, game over (B cell at (4, 6))
So I can't go LEFT from (5, 7) to (5, 5). I need to find another way to get to the left side.
From (5, 7), I can go:
- DOWN to (7, 7): valid
- UP to (3, 7): valid
From (7, 7), I can go:
- LEFT to (7, 5): might cause game over (B cell at (6, 6))
- RIGHT to (7, 9): back
From (7, 5), I can go:
- LEFT to (7, 3): (7, 3) doesn't exist. Invalid.
- DOWN to (9, 5): (9, 5) doesn't exist. Invalid.
- UP to (5, 5): valid? Let me check. Intermediate (6, 5) = g, dest (5, 5) = W. Valid.
But wait, the LEFT from (7, 7) to (7, 5) might cause a game over because it's near the B cell at (6, 6). Let me check the distance. (7, 7) to (7, 5) passes through (7, 6) = g. The B cell at (6, 6) is 1 row above (7, 6). So the animation bounding box would include (6, 6), which is a B cell. This might cause a game over.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. The player needs to get from the right side (col 9) to the left side (col 3). The only way to do this is to go through row 5, which has W cells from col -1 to 11. But the LEFT moves on row 5 near col 5-7 cause game overs due to the B cell at (4, 6).
Wait, let me re-examine the level 2 solution. In level 2, the player went:
- UP from (6, -1) to (4, -1)
- RIGHT from (4, -1) to (4, 1)
- RIGHT from (4, 1) to (4, 3)
- DOWN from (4, 3) to (6, 3)
- RIGHT from (6, 3) to (6, 5)
- RIGHT from (6, 5) to (6, 7)
- UP from (6, 7) to (4, 7) [red trap]
- RIGHT from (4, 7) to (4, 9)
- RIGHT from (4, 9) to (4, 11)
- UP from (4, 11) to (2, 11) [target]
The key insight is that the player avoided the problematic RIGHT from (4, 3) to (4, 5) by going DOWN to row 6, then RIGHT to (6, 7), then UP to (4, 7). This avoided the horizontal move near the red trap at (4, 7).
In level 3, I need to avoid the horizontal moves near the B cell at (4, 6) and the red traps at (3, 3) and (3, 5). 
Let me think about the path again. The player is at (9, 9) and the target is at (9, 3). The player needs to go from the right side to the left side.
One approach is to go around the B cells and red traps by going to the top or bottom. But the top (row 1-2) and bottom (row 9) don't have continuous paths.
Another approach is to go through row 7, which has W cells at cols 1, 5, 7, 9. From (7, 9), I can go LEFT to (7, 7), then LEFT to (7, 5). But the LEFT from (7, 7) to (7, 5) might cause a game over due to the B cell at (6, 6).
Let me try this path: UP, LEFT, LEFT, UP, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT.
(9, 9) -> (7, 9) -> (7, 7) -> (7, 5) -> (5, 5) -> (5, 3) -> (5, 1) -> (7, 1) -> (9, 1) -> (9, 3)
But wait, the LEFT from (7, 7) to (7, 5) might cause a game over. Let me test it first.
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The game over in level 3 happened on:
1. LEFT from (5, 7) to (5, 5): 15 frames, game over. B cell at (4, 6) is 1 row above.
2. LEFT from (3, 7) to (3, 5): 17 frames, game over. Red traps at (3, 3) and (3, 5), B cells at (2, 4), (2, 6), (4, 6).
The LEFT from (7, 7) to (7, 5) would have the B cell at (6, 6) 1 row above. This is similar to the LEFT from (5, 7) to (5, 5), which had the B cell at (4, 6) 1 row above. So it might also cause a game over.
Hmm, let me try a different approach. What if I go DOWN from (5, 7) to (7, 7), then LEFT to (7, 5), then UP to (5, 5)? This would avoid the horizontal move near the B cell at (4, 6), but the LEFT from (7, 7) to (7, 5) might still cause a game over due to the B cell at (6, 6).
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the level 2 solution. The player went DOWN from (4, 3) to (6, 3), which is a vertical move. Vertical moves don't seem to cause game overs, even when near red traps or B cells.
So the rule is: horizontal moves near red traps or B cells cause game overs, but vertical moves are fine.
In level 3, from (5, 7), I can go DOWN to (7, 7), which is a vertical move. Then from (7, 7), I can go LEFT to (7, 5), which is a horizontal move near the B cell at (6, 6). This might cause a game over.
But wait, in level 2, the player went RIGHT from (6, 3) to (6, 5) and RIGHT from (6, 5) to (6, 7), which are horizontal moves. These didn't cause game overs because they're not near any red traps or B cells.
In level 3, the B cells are at (2, 4), (2, 6), (4, 6), (6, 0), (6, 6), (6, 8). The red traps are at (3, 3), (3, 5), (7, -1).
The LEFT from (7, 7) to (7, 5) passes through (7, 6) = g. The B cell at (6, 6) is 1 row above (7, 6). So the animation bounding box would include (6, 6), which is a B cell. This might cause a game over.
But the LEFT from (5, 9) to (5, 7) passed through (5, 8) = g. The B cell at (4, 6) is 2 rows above and 2 columns to the left of (5, 8). So the animation bounding box might not include (4, 6), which is why it didn't cause a game over.
Hmm, the distance matters. Let me think about the animation bounding boxes:
- LEFT from (5, 9) to (5, 7): bounding box is 'top-left (30,36) 3x8, 24 cells'. This covers grid rows 5-5 and cols 7-9 (approximately). The B cell at (4, 6) is outside this bounding box.
- LEFT from (5, 7) to (5, 5): bounding box is 'top-left (24,28) 11x10, 54 cells'. This covers grid rows 3-5 and cols 3-7 (approximately). The B cell at (4, 6) is inside this bounding box.
So the animation bounding box expands when the move is near red traps or B cells. When the bounding box includes a red trap or B cell, the animation becomes 15+ frames and causes a game over.
The LEFT from (7, 7) to (7, 5) would have a bounding box that includes (6, 6), which is a B cell. So it might cause a game over.
Let me try a different approach. What if I go from (5, 7) DOWN to (7, 7), then LEFT to (7, 5), and see if it causes a game over? If it does, I'll need to find another path.
Actually, I realize I should try the path UP, LEFT, LEFT from the start (9, 9) and see if the LEFT from (7, 7) to (7, 5) causes a game over. If it does, I'll know that horizontal moves near B cells cause game overs, regardless of the row.
Let me try: UP, LEFT, LEFT from (9, 9).
(9, 9) -> (7, 9) -> (7, 7) -> (7, 5)
If the LEFT from (7, 7) to (7, 5) causes a game over, then I need to find a path that avoids horizontal moves near B cells.
But wait, the player is currently at (9, 9) because the game over reset the player to the start. Let me verify this by checking the current frame.
Actually, looking at the image, the player (blue dot) is at (9, 9), which is the start position. So the game over reset the player to the start.
Let me try the path UP, LEFT, LEFT from (9, 9) and see if the LEFT from (7, 7) to (7, 5) causes a game over.

Plan: Test if LEFT from (7,7) to (7,5) also causes game over (near B cell at (6,6)). Try UP, LEFT, LEFT from start.
